senses.definition: A traditional Moroccan house or small hotel built around a central courtyard or garden, often used for tourist accommodation.
examples: We stayed in a beautiful riad in Marrakech with a small pool in the courtyard.
Many old riads have been turned into boutique hotels for visitors.
From the roof terrace of the riad, you could see the whole medina.

senses.definition: Plural of riad: traditional Moroccan houses or small hotels built around a central courtyard or garden.
examples: There are many riads hidden in the narrow streets of the old city.
Online you can compare different riads before you book.
The tour offers accommodation in family-run riads.

senses.definition: The unit of money used in Iran and Oman.
examples: The price was listed in rial, not in dollars.
The value of the rial has fallen against the euro.
She exchanged her dollars for Iranian rial at the airport.

senses.definition: Plural of rial: units of money used in Iran and Oman.
examples: The meal cost about 200,000 rials.
He changed his euros into rials before flying to Tehran.
Prices in the market are usually given in rials.

senses.definition: The main business or theater district of a city, especially the area with many theaters or entertainment venues.
examples: They opened a small bar in the city’s rialto, close to the main theaters.
In old films, characters often met in the smoky rialto after the show.
The rialto was busy every night during the festival.

senses.definition: An area in Venice, Italy, that includes a famous bridge and the old commercial center of the city.
examples: We walked over the Rialto and took photos of the Grand Canal.
Shops near the Rialto were crowded with tourists.
They booked a hotel just a few minutes from the Rialto.

senses.definition: Plural of rialto: main business or theater districts of cities, or references to such areas.
examples: The old rialtos of European cities are now packed with tourists.
Writers often describe the rialtos of their time as noisy and colorful.
These streets were once the rialtos where traders met every day.

senses.definition: Smiling or laughing, or looking bright and cheerful, especially in a literary style.
examples: Her riant face lit up the whole room.
They spent a riant evening telling stories and jokes.
Fields and gardens lay riant in the summer sun.

senses.definition: In a smiling, cheerful, or laughing way, especially in a literary style.
examples: She laughed riantly at his playful remark.
Children ran riantly through the garden.
The sun shone riantly over the quiet town.

senses.definition: Plural of ria: long, narrow sea inlets formed when river valleys are flooded by the sea.
examples: The coast is broken by many deep rias.
Fishermen often work in the sheltered waters of the rias.
Tourists enjoy boat trips along the rias and cliffs.

senses.definition: A long rope with a loop at one end, used mainly in North and Central America for catching cattle and horses.
examples: The cowboy swung his riata and caught the running calf.
He hung his worn riata on a nail in the barn.
Old photos showed vaqueros riding with their riata ready.

senses.definition: Plural of riata: long ropes with loops used for catching cattle and horses.
examples: The barn was full of saddles, boots, and old riatas.
Skilled riders practiced with their riatas every day.
Different riatas were used for different kinds of work.

senses.definition: One of the curved bones in the chest that protect the lungs and heart.
examples: He broke a rib when he fell off his bike.
She had a pain between her ribs when she coughed.
The doctor said his cracked rib would heal in a few weeks.

senses.definition: A piece of meat that includes one of these bones, eaten as food.
examples: We had barbecued ribs for dinner.
The restaurant is famous for its spicy pork ribs.
She ordered a half rack of ribs and a salad.

senses.definition: A long curved supporting part in something like a boat, umbrella, or roof frame.
examples: The ribs of the umbrella snapped in the strong wind.
The boat’s wooden ribs were clearly visible.
Steel ribs support the curved roof of the stadium.

senses.definition: A line or raised band in cloth or knitted fabric.
examples: The sweater has narrow ribs at the cuffs.
Ribs in the fabric make it stretch more easily.
She chose a dress with fine vertical ribs.

senses.definition: To joke with someone in a friendly way, often by making fun of them a little.
examples: They ribbed him about his new haircut.
She likes to rib her brother when he gets nervous.
Don’t take it so seriously, we’re just ribbing you.

senses.definition: In Islamic law, the practice of charging interest on loans, which is considered wrong or forbidden by many scholars.
examples: Some Muslims avoid bank accounts that involve riba.
The contract was rejected because it contained elements of riba.
Islamic finance aims to structure products that do not involve riba.

senses.definition: Referring to sex in a funny but quite rude or shocking way.
examples: The comedian’s ribald jokes made some people uncomfortable.
They exchanged ribald comments about the scene in the movie.
The book is full of ribald humor and wild adventures.

senses.definition: A person who makes jokes or comments about sex in a rude or shocking way.
examples: At parties he plays the ribald, always telling dirty stories.
The poet was seen as a charming ribald in his day.
The film portrays kings, saints, and ribalds side by side.

senses.definition: More ribald; containing ruder or more shocking sexual humor.
examples: His later plays are ribalder than his early work.
The jokes became ribalder as the night went on.
Critics found this book ribalder and less subtle than the first.

senses.definition: Most ribald; containing the rudest or most shocking sexual humor.
examples: That was the ribaldest joke I’ve ever heard on television.
Critics called it his ribaldest and most daring performance.
Of all his stories, this one is the ribaldest.

senses.definition: Somewhat ribald; slightly rude or shocking in a sexual way but not very strong.
examples: The show includes a few ribaldish jokes.
Her stories were light, fast, and a bit ribaldish.
They laughed at his ribaldish comments and relaxed.

senses.definition: In a way that is ribald, with rude or shocking sexual humor.
examples: They laughed ribaldly at the suggestive scene.
He spoke ribaldly, making the audience blush.
The characters joke ribaldly throughout the play.

senses.definition: The quality of being ribald, with rude or shocking sexual humor.
examples: Some viewers were offended by the film’s ribaldness.
His stories are full of cheerful ribaldness.
The novel mixes serious ideas with moments of pure ribaldness.

senses.definition: Plural of ribaldry: rude or shocking sexual jokes, stories, or behavior.
examples: The play is full of songs and ribaldries.
They traded ribaldries late into the night.
Her letters contain humorous ribaldries about court life.

senses.definition: Very ribald; full of rude or shocking sexual humor, especially in older or literary use.
examples: The play’s ribaldrous scenes shocked early audiences.
He was famous for his ribaldrous ballads and sharp tongue.
A ribaldrous mood took over the tavern as the night grew late.

senses.definition: Language or behavior that is funny but quite rude or shocking, especially about sex.
examples: The show mixes sharp wit with a lot of ribaldry.
Some viewers complained about the level of ribaldry in the program.
Medieval festivals often included songs full of ribaldry.

senses.definition: Plural of ribald: people who make rude or shocking sexual jokes.
examples: The story tells of kings, priests, and ribalds sharing the same table.
At the back of the hall, a few ribalds laughed loudly.
The poet mocked both the powerful and the ribalds of the street.

senses.definition: An old or literary word for a narrow strip of cloth, used for tying or as decoration, especially as a sign of honor.
examples: She wore a blue riband in her hair.
A bright riband was pinned to his coat as a prize.
Soldiers marched with colored ribands on their uniforms.

senses.definition: Long, narrow, and flat like a riband or ribbon.
examples: A ribandlike stream wound through the valley.
She followed a ribandlike path of light across the floor.
The river appeared ribandlike from the hilltop.

senses.definition: Someone whose work is to make ribands or ribbons, especially in older times.
examples: Her grandfather had been a ribandmaker in the town.
The guild of ribandmakers was once very powerful here.
Old records list tailors, shoemakers, and ribandmakers.

senses.definition: The making, selling, or use of ribands or ribbons, especially as a trade in older times.
examples: Ribandry was an important industry in the town.
Her family had been involved in ribandry for generations.
The decline of ribandry hurt many small workshops.

senses.definition: Plural of riband: narrow strips of cloth used for tying, decoration, or as signs of honor.
examples: Children wore colored ribands for the festival.
Her hair was tied back with two white ribands.
The medals hung from bright ribands on his chest.

senses.definition: In Islamic history, a small fort, monastery, or religious building where soldiers or religious people lived, often near a frontier.
examples: The old ribat guarded the coast for centuries.
Pilgrims rested at a ribat on their long journey.
Historians have studied the role of ribats in spreading learning.

senses.definition: In music, a repeated or echoed rhythmic figure or phrase, especially in older Italian musical terms.
examples: The composer marks a short ribattuta in the violin part.
Singers must match the rhythm exactly in the ribattuta.
The ribattuta gives the piece a playful, bouncing feel.

senses.definition: Plural of ribattuta: repeated or echoed rhythmic figures or phrases in music.
examples: The piece is full of playful ribattutas.
Different instruments take up the ribattutas in turn.
The composer uses ribattutas to build excitement.

senses.definition: An old term for a person who behaves in a rude, sexually immoral, or noisy way.
examples: The king banished the ribauds from his court.
Stories speak of ribauds drinking and fighting in the streets.
She warned her son not to mix with the town ribauds.

senses.definition: A medieval war machine with several small gun barrels on a frame, used to fire many shots at once.
examples: The army placed a ribaudequin at the city gate.
Historians describe the ribaudequin as an early form of volley gun.
The noise of the ribaudequin terrified the soldiers.

senses.definition: Plural of ribaudry: rude or sexually immoral acts or jokes, especially in older texts.
examples: The poem lists the ribaudries of the court.
He entertained the crowd with songs and ribaudries.
Moralists wrote against the ribaudries of the city.

senses.definition: Rude, noisy, or sexually immoral behavior or talk, especially in older writing.
examples: He was known for his songs full of ribaudry.
The preacher warned the people against ribaudry and drink.
Tales of ribaudry and feasting were popular at the time.

senses.definition: Plural of ribaud: people who behave in rude or sexually immoral ways.
examples: The king ordered the ribauds out of the hall.
Songs tell of ribauds drinking in the taverns.
He preferred the company of poets to that of ribauds.

senses.definition: A medicine used as an antiviral drug, often given with other drugs to treat certain virus infections such as hepatitis C.
examples: The doctor prescribed ribavirin as part of his treatment.
Ribavirin can have serious side effects in some patients.
They studied the effect of ribavirin on different viruses.

senses.definition: Plural of ribavirin: doses or forms of the antiviral drug ribavirin.
examples: Different ribavirins were tested in the clinical trial.
The study compared ribavirins from several manufacturers.
They reported that some ribavirins caused fewer side effects.

senses.definition: The hard, heavy tooth of a hippopotamus, used as ivory.
examples: Traders once sold ribazuba along the coast.
The market displayed ribazuba carved into small figures.
Hunting bans helped reduce the trade in ribazuba.

senses.definition: An alternative spelling of riband: a narrow strip of cloth used for tying or as decoration.
examples: She tied her hair with a red ribband.
The medal hung from a blue ribband.
They decorated the hall with colorful ribbands.

senses.definition: The trade, craft, or use of ribbands or ribbons, especially in older times.
examples: Ribbandry brought work to many families in the town.
The museum shows tools once used in ribbandry.
He wrote about the decline of local ribbandry.

senses.definition: Plural of ribband: narrow strips of cloth used for tying, decoration, or as signs of honor.
examples: She bought several ribbands to decorate the costumes.
Children danced with long ribbands in their hands.
The chest was tied with faded ribbands.

senses.definition: Having raised lines or ridges along the surface.
examples: He wore a ribbed sweater to keep warm.
The jar has a ribbed surface so it is easier to hold.
Ribbed socks stay up better on your legs.
